summary:
For a diagnosis of mild sleep apnea, the AHI index ranges from 5 to 14 events per hour; options indicating values higher than 14 are incorrect based on established medical criteria. To diagnose mildly severe cases accurately requires adherence strictly within these parameters as defined clinically. 

End Summary
To clarify further,

The critical point here involves understanding how AHI scores categorize severity levels—specifically recognizing what constitutes 'mild' versus more serious conditions through their respective numerical thresholds. Thus ensuring diagnostic precision hinges upon knowing where each category begins and ends along this continuum measured via hourly event counts during nighttime respiration patterns observed medically. 
This summary encapsulates both core aspects needed—the specific numeric boundaries defining mild vs other severities—and emphasizes why those limits matter contextually when assessing patient care needs related directly back towards accurate diagnoses made possible using standardized scoring methods such as AHI indices across various degrees of obstructive or central types encountered commonly amongst patients presenting symptoms suggestive thereof at primary healthcare settings today globally speaking broadly enough without overcomplicating matters unnecessarily while still conveying essential information comprehensively yet succinctly too. End Clarification Note
In conclusion:

For diagnosing mild sleep apnea specifically, one must look closely at whether the average number of breathing pauses falls into the narrow band specified exactly between five up until but excluding
